But you realize they are such different platforms, specifically with Snapchat, because it is about interaction and messaging. It is not an entertainment-for-entertainment-sake app like YouTube is. We don't take YouTube to update our closest friends on what's happening. YouTube is also not about speed. When the fires were going on, Snap was actually a really visual resource to see what was going on. The map itself is a resource to see who is posting from the Palisades right now, and you click in and see what it looks like in that exact moment. When I think about TikTok, I think we compare TikTok and YouTube so much because they are both lean-back experiences in a way. That's true—those two are actually really similar. It's like I go there for entertainment primarily, watching, not interacting. Instagram and Snap are more similar in that they are messaging. I'm messaging my friends on Instagram, sending them content, connecting with them. On Snap, you're messaging and then engaging with creators like David Dobrik. If you want to see what a Snap creator looks like right now, just watch David Dobrik's stories. That's what Snap creation, I think, to the masses really looks like. But that content does not look different from your friend's content. On Instagram, my friend's content looks really different from creator content, but that's not the case on Snap.
turns.zh: 酷。谢谢，Evan。聊得很开心。我的意思是，在节目之前、期间和之后，我们都和 Evan 进行了很棒的对话。我认为创作者和 Snap 的关系一直不是线性的，我想这是因为 Snap 是一个你出于多种原因打开的 App，可能主要是为了和朋友互动。所以一个你打开是为了和朋友互动、同时上面也有创作者的平台，是一个非常不同的平台。很难把它和 YouTube 相比。我甚至问了一个问题：YouTube 在电视上的观看时长在增长，Snapchat 是否对此感兴趣？听起来并不像。甚至很难把 Snapchat 这样的 App 和 YouTube 相提并论。我认为我们在短视频 App 上经常这样做——试图把 Reels 和 TikTok 与 YouTube 比较，因为我们显然非常以 YouTube 为原生。但你会意识到它们是如此不同的平台，尤其是 Snapchat，因为它关乎互动和消息。它不是一个像 YouTube 那样为了娱乐而娱乐的 App。我们不会用 YouTube 来向最亲密的朋友更新动态。YouTube 也不关乎速度。当火灾发生时，Snap 实际上是一个非常有视觉感的资源，可以看到发生了什么。地图本身就是一个资源，可以看到谁正在从 Palisades 发帖，点进去就能看到那个时刻的样子。当我想到 TikTok 时，我认为我们之所以经常比较 TikTok 和 YouTube，是因为它们在某种程度上都是后仰式体验。确实如此——这两个其实非常相似。我去那里主要是为了娱乐，观看，而不是互动。Instagram 和 Snap 更相似，因为它们都是消息类 App。我在 Instagram 上给朋友发消息，发内容，和他们联系。在 Snap 上，你发消息，然后和像 David Dobrik 这样的创作者互动。如果你想知道现在的 Snap 创作者是什么样子，就看 David Dobrik 的故事。我认为这就是大众眼中 Snap 创作的样子。但那些内容和朋友的内容看起来没什么不同。在 Instagram 上，我朋友的内容和创作者的内容看起来非常不同，但在 Snap 上不是这样。

turns.en: In terms of production value, visually, a Snapchat piece of content looks like a Snapchat piece of content. I think it's just because of the emphasis on the camera opening to the camera, and making sure people try and use the phone camera maintains a certain aesthetic. To Evan's point, it maintains a certain level of authenticity. I've always been interested in posting content on Snap, but I've always been a bit overwhelmed by it. I don't know why. Cutting content for it has just never been top of mind. But over the past year, I've engaged with so many more people from the company, talked to more creators who are utilizing the platform. I think the world of vertical video, with potentially whatever is going to happen with TikTok, is about to shift in different ways. Snap is one of those platforms that really encourages an authenticity that is starting to get lost on social media. I've brought up this quote probably ad nauseam, but when Jake Shane said to us that the best TikToks feel like you're on FaceTime with a friend. Does ad nauseam mean it should make you nauseous? Because I've said it so much that you might feel nauseous. Oh, I think I understand ad nauseam because I bring it up a lot. But I think Snapchat is like that to the extreme. It really still retains what social media originally was: just this unfiltered look into human beings' lives. So thinking about the future of platforms and creator platforms, I think YouTube will continue to go in the direction of competing with Netflix and competing with this sit-down-on-your-couch-and-watch-premium-programming. Now where we all engage with each other, and that mix of shopping—he said something in the episode about shopping, that Snap actually has more influence on shopping than other platforms. I believe that because purchase decisions are made through word of mouth, and production value creates a barrier between me and word of mouth. It no longer feels like word of mouth. But again, you watch Dobrik's snaps and you're like, yeah, he's just talking into a phone camera. Not to say that creator ads perform a lot better than ads made by the companies. If you look at most of the ads on Snap, something that I enjoy that I don't think other platforms do well is that most Snap ads are shot through like they look like they're from the Snap camera. Everything on that platform feels native, and that's really interesting. That's not the case on YouTube or Instagram. So that's really interesting to me, and I'm just really curious to see where the company goes.
turns.zh: 从制作价值来看，视觉上，Snapchat 上的内容看起来就是 Snapchat 风格的内容。我认为这是因为强调相机打开到相机，并确保人们尝试使用手机相机，这保持了一种特定的美学。正如 Evan 所说，它保持了一定程度的真实性。我一直对在 Snap 上发布内容感兴趣，但总是有点不知所措。不知道为什么。为它剪辑内容从来都不是我的首要任务。但在过去一年里，我和公司里更多的人接触了，和更多利用这个平台的创作者聊了。我认为竖屏视频的世界，随着 TikTok 可能发生的变化，即将以不同的方式转变。Snap 是那些真正鼓励真实性的平台之一，而这种真实性在社交媒体上正开始消失。我可能已经引用过这句话无数次了，但 Jake Shane 对我们说过，最好的 TikTok 感觉就像你在和 FaceTime 朋友聊天。Ad nauseam 的意思是它应该让你恶心吗？因为我说了太多次，你可能觉得恶心了。哦，我想我明白 ad nauseam 的意思了，因为我经常提起它。但我认为 Snapchat 在这方面做到了极致。它仍然保留了社交媒体最初的样子：对人类生活不加滤镜的窥视。所以思考平台和创作者平台的未来，我认为 YouTube 将继续朝着与 Netflix 竞争的方向发展，与这种坐在沙发上看优质节目的体验竞争。现在我们彼此互动，还有购物的混合——他在节目中提到了购物，说 Snap 实际上比其他平台对购物有更大的影响力。我相信这一点，因为购买决策是通过口碑做出的，而制作价值在我和口碑之间制造了障碍。它不再像口碑了。但再看 Dobrik 的 snaps，你会觉得，是啊，他只是在对着手机摄像头说话。并不是说创作者广告比公司制作的广告表现好很多。如果你看 Snap 上的大多数广告，我喜欢的一点是，其他平台做得不好的是，大多数 Snap 广告看起来就像是用 Snap 相机拍摄的。那个平台上的一切都感觉很原生，这真的很有趣。在 YouTube 或 Instagram 上就不是这样。所以这对我来说真的很有趣，我很好奇这家公司会走向何方。

turns.en: because they've had an emphasis on wearables for 10 years now, yeah. So it's like, is that the direction? And every company now has an emphasis on wearables.
turns.zh: 因为他们已经专注可穿戴设备 10 年了，对吧。所以问题是，这是未来的方向吗？现在每家公司都在强调可穿戴设备。

turns.en: Absolutely, it's the direction it seems like. And you know, we've talked about this before where we've been in rooms and seen a lot of demos and a lot of interesting things, but it's hard to wrap your head around like, is this really about to happen? But it's interesting to see that these investments are being made in the direction of like a bet on 10, 15 years from now.
turns.zh: 绝对是的，这看起来就是方向。而且我们之前也聊过，我们参加过很多演示会，见过很多有趣的东西，但很难真正理解——这真的会发生吗？但有趣的是，这些投资都是朝着押注未来 10 到 15 年的方向去的。

turns.en: I appreciated the realism with which he talked about looking at screens being harmful, right? Like he has a product for our screens where we look at our screens, but it seemed like as a parent and with the information that's out there, he has the understanding that yeah, no, like this could be harmful to our eyes. Like we don't know. This phone consumption is actually such a new thing, and the right thing to do is to rethink what a computer is. And a computer does not just have to be a screen. A computer should be something that fits more. Maybe I'm just drinking the wearables Kool-Aid over here, but I do hear that and I'm like, yeah, I think you're right. Like we should push the bounds of what a computer is. We shouldn't just think about looking at these screens and hunching over. And should think about, you know, like in 15 years, do our kids actually be running around again? Because they don't—not because we're asking them to run around and play outside, but because they don't feel like they have the reliance on the phone.
turns.zh: 我很欣赏他谈论看屏幕有害时的现实主义态度。他有一个让我们盯着屏幕的产品，但作为家长，根据现有的信息，他理解这确实可能对眼睛有害。我们不知道。手机消费其实是一件很新的事情，正确的做法是重新思考计算机是什么。计算机不一定非得是一块屏幕。计算机应该是一种更贴合我们的东西。也许我只是被可穿戴设备的宣传洗脑了，但我听到这些时，确实觉得你说得对。我们应该拓展计算机的边界。我们不应该只想着盯着屏幕、弯腰驼背。而应该想想，15 年后，我们的孩子是不是真的能再次自由奔跑？不是因为我们叫他们去外面跑着玩，而是因为他们不再觉得离不开手机了。

turns.en: But also, you know, the reality is the reason we should rethink that is because computers have not been a part of our lives for that long. Like we experienced at our age, we've experienced going from computers not really being a part of our life to computers being a part of your life on a minute-by-minute basis. Mhm. And you know, now it's going to be even further, right? We're going to be like almost—we're moving in a direction where we're becoming like one with computers. Yeah, and hopefully that's the right thing. Hopefully, right? Because like what he said there was, we because technology was advancing and it became so good, we conformed as humans to what the technology could do, right? By putting it in these boxes, and we had to operate the technology in a certain type of way. And that hopefully the paradigm is shifting to technology and computers that work more with who we actually are and how our natural—yeah, our natural state.
turns.zh: 但另一方面，现实是我们应该重新思考的原因是，计算机进入我们的生活并没有那么久。像我们这一代人，经历了从计算机几乎不参与生活，到计算机每分钟都参与生活的过程。嗯。而现在，这还会更进一步，对吧？我们几乎要走向与计算机融为一体的方向。希望这是件好事。希望如此，对吧？因为就像他说的，因为技术在进步，变得如此之好，我们人类反而去适应技术能做什么——把它放进这些盒子里，我们不得不以某种特定方式操作技术。而希望范式正在转变，转向更符合我们真实自我和自然状态的技术和计算机。

turns.en: That's the optimistic lens. I think one part of our natural state as humans has been, you know, our desire to and our ability to tell each other's stories. And so I just wonder how that will take shape as time moves on, because I think actually technology has amplified and supported our ability to tell each other stories, right? At scale. At scale. And now we're seeing that—I mean, that is also a spectrum of all kinds of good and bad. One thing Evan mentioned is that simplicity in storytelling is what really works, and that's why we are more susceptible to conspiracy theories, because they boil a very complex topic into a very simple thing. So I thought that was really interesting too, that technology has supported our ability to tell stories to each other, that has many edges—it can send good and bad stories very far.
turns.zh: 这是乐观的视角。我认为我们人类自然状态的一部分，就是我们渴望并且有能力互相讲述故事。所以我很好奇，随着时间推移，这会如何演变，因为我认为技术实际上放大并支持了我们互相讲故事的能力，对吧？大规模地。大规模地。现在我们看到——那也是一个包含各种好与坏的频谱。Evan 提到的一点是，讲故事的简洁性才是真正有效的，这就是为什么我们更容易相信阴谋论，因为它们把一个非常复杂的话题简化成非常简单的说法。所以我觉得那也很有趣，技术支持了我们互相讲故事的能力，这有很多面——它可以把好故事和坏故事都传播得很远。

turns.en: Exactly. And so now, this next version of computing being even more ubiquitous with the human experience, where does storytelling fit into that? How does storytelling now permeate through human existence when we are constantly plugged into something? And can technology be used to make sure we know what we're looking at is verifiably real? Right? Because the human experience is to see something in front of your eyes and be able to tell someone, 'No, I saw it with my eyes.' Of course conspiracies can start there too, but at least you know you believe something when you see it in front of your eyes. That's what he was talking about with Snap Map—that at least, hey, at least you know for the most part that this video was taken in this location with the camera on the phone.
turns.zh: 完全正确。那么现在，计算的下一个版本与人类体验更加密不可分，讲故事在其中扮演什么角色？当我们一直与某种东西连接时，讲故事如何渗透到人类存在中？技术能否用来确保我们看到的东西是可验证的真实？对吧？因为人类的体验是亲眼看到某样东西，然后能告诉别人：“不，我亲眼看到的。”当然，阴谋论也可以从那里开始，但至少当你亲眼看到时，你会相信某件事。这就是他谈到 Snap Map 时说的——至少，嘿，至少你大致知道这个视频是在这个位置用手机摄像头拍摄的。

turns.en: Big questions. Yeah, big questions. It's just fascinating to me, you know, that the young creative people from the past decade-plus are the people solving for that. Isn't that interesting? Like Evan Spiegel, Mark Zuckerberg, yeah. Like you make an app to connect your friends, and then you're on a desktop, yeah, on a desktop. And then, you know, fast forward 15 years later, and you're talking about how humans should engage with computers in general. I guess it makes sense—the dots connect when you look backwards, but it's amazing to me that, you know, we're a show that talks about the creator economy, but that the creator economy is like humans telling stories to each other. And the future of media and the future of trust and the future of it is so much bigger than I think what I thought the creator economy was. The underlying subtext is like, is all of this good for humans or bad? Right? And of course it's a gradient, there are shades of gray. Yeah. But that also is this underlying conversation of the way that we're now able to tell stories—is it good, is it bad, how do we make it safe and just better for everyone? The immediacy at which we can get information out to each other—is that positive or negative, or what does the spectrum look like?
turns.zh: 大问题。是啊，大问题。我觉得很神奇的是，过去十多年的年轻创意人才正是解决这些问题的人。这不有趣吗？比如 Evan Spiegel、Mark Zuckerberg。你做了一个连接朋友的 app，然后你是在桌面上用的。然后，15 年后，你在谈论人类应该如何与计算机互动。我想这说得通——回头看时，这些点连起来了，但让我惊讶的是，我们是一个谈论创作者经济的节目，但创作者经济其实就是人类互相讲故事。而媒体的未来、信任的未来、以及整个未来，比我原来以为的创作者经济要大得多。潜在的潜台词是：这一切对人类是好是坏？对吧？当然这是一个渐变，有灰色地带。是的。但这也是一场潜在的对话：我们现在能够讲故事的方式——是好是坏，我们如何让它安全、对每个人都更好？我们能够如此即时地向彼此传递信息——这是积极的还是消极的，或者这个频谱是什么样的？

turns.en: So apparently we've left you with more questions than answers. Yeah, hopefully that's a good thing. So hopefully you guys enjoyed this conversation. It was really fun for us. We'd love to hear your thoughts on this conversation with Evan on Snapchat, on the future of human connection. All right, make sure you subscribe and we'll see you on the next episode of The Colin and Samir Show.
turns.zh: 所以显然我们给你留下的问题比答案还多。是的，希望这是件好事。希望你们喜欢这次对话。我们觉得非常有趣。我们很想知道你们对这次与 Evan 在 Snapchat 上关于人类连接未来的对话的看法。好了，记得订阅，我们下期《Colin and Samir Show》再见。

consensus.en: Authentic connection with audience transcends any platform change.
consensus.zh: 与受众的真实联系超越任何平台变化。

consensus.en: Opening to camera fosters broad creativity by lowering comparison pressure.
consensus.zh: 打开相机通过降低比较压力来促进广泛的创造力。

consensus.en: Ephemerality mirrors natural conversation, lowering barriers to expression.
consensus.zh: 短暂性模仿自然对话，降低表达门槛。

consensus.en: Future of computing is glasses that keep people grounded in real world.
consensus.zh: 计算的未来是让人们扎根于现实世界的眼镜。

consensus.en: Snapchat's core value is visual communication with friends and family.
consensus.zh: Snapchat 的核心价值是与朋友和家人的视觉交流。

consensus.en: TikTok ban debate intensified after its influence on US president.
consensus.zh: TikTok 禁令辩论因其对美国总统的影响力而加剧。

contrarian.en: Copying by competitors validates innovation and motivates harder-to-copy work.
contrarian.zh: 竞争对手的抄袭验证了创新，并激励更难复制的工作。

contrarian.en: Opening to feed can stifle creativity due to popularity ranking comparison.
contrarian.zh: 打开信息流会因流行度排名比较而扼杀创造力。

contrarian.en: TikTok's success came from subsidizing both creators and viewers, plus trends.
contrarian.zh: TikTok 的成功源于补贴创作者和观众，以及趋势。

contrarian.en: Snapchat's ad revenue share works better for longer-form content with mid-roll ads.
contrarian.zh: Snapchat 的广告收入分成更适合带有中插广告的长内容。

contrarian.en: First Amendment allows platforms to choose content guidelines, not mandate free speech.
contrarian.zh: 第一修正案允许平台选择内容准则，而非强制言论自由。

contrarian.en: Wearables like Spectacles aim to solve screen addiction, not just add another screen.
contrarian.zh: 像 Spectacles 这样的可穿戴设备旨在解决屏幕成瘾，而不仅仅是增加另一个屏幕。
